content.text: A generative model learns the joint distribution p(x, y) or, in unsupervised settings, the marginal distribution p(x) over inputs. Rather than drawing a decision boundary between classes, it builds a statistical model of how data is generated — what values of x are plausible, what combinations of features tend to co-occur, and how likely any given input is. This generative view unlocks capabilities that discriminative models cannot provide: sampling new data, reasoning under missing observations, and detecting anomalies through low likelihood.
A generative model explicitly represents p(x, y) = p(x | y) · p(y). Bayes' theorem then recovers the conditional needed for classification:

attrs.latex: p(y \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid y)\, p(y)}{p(x)} = \frac{p(x \mid y)\, p(y)}{\sum_{y'} p(x \mid y')\, p(y')}

content.text: In unsupervised settings there are no labels and the model learns p(x) directly. Regardless of which form is used, the defining feature is that p(x) — the probability of the input itself — is part of the model. This is what enables generation: sampling x ~ p(x) or x ~ p(x | y = cat).
Training a generative model on a dataset {x_i} means maximising the marginal log-likelihood:

This objective requires assigning high probability to training data and, implicitly, lower probability to everything else. The model must account for all the variation in x: for images, this means modelling textures, shapes, lighting, and composition simultaneously. This is a much harder problem than learning a decision boundary, which explains why generative models typically require more data and compute than discriminative ones to reach comparable classification accuracy.
Generative models differ in how they represent and compute p(x). Three fundamental families:
The most principled way to define p(x) for sequential data is the chain rule factorisation:

This is exactly what GPT-style language models learn. Each factor p(x_t | x_{<t}) is modelled by a neural network (Transformer), and training maximises the sum of log-factors over all positions and all sequences. The likelihood is tractable because the product decomposes into tractable per-token conditionals. Generation samples x_t ~ p(x_t | x_{<t}) autoregressively from left to right.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch import Tensor


class SimpleAutoregressiveGenerativeModel(nn.Module):
    """
    Autoregressive generative model: factorises p(x) = prod_t p(x_t | x_{<t}).
    Learns the joint distribution p(x) via conditional next-token prediction.
    Unlike a discriminative classifier, the training signal is the data
    distribution itself — no labels needed.
    """
    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, d_model: int = 256, n_layers: int = 4):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
        self.pos_embed = nn.Embedding(512, d_model)
        encoder_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            d_model, nhead=4, batch_first=True
        )
        self.transformer = nn.TransformerEncoder(encoder_layer, n_layers)
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(d_model, vocab_size)

    def forward(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """Returns logits for p(x_t | x_{<t}) at every position."""
        T = x.size(1)
        positions = torch.arange(T, device=x.device)
        h = self.embed(x) + self.pos_embed(positions)
        # causal mask: each position attends only to past tokens
        mask = nn.Transformer.generate_square_subsequent_mask(T, device=x.device)
        h = self.transformer(h, mask=mask, is_causal=True)
        return self.lm_head(h)   # (B, T, vocab_size)

    def nll(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """
        Negative log-likelihood: -log p(x) = sum_t -log p(x_t | x_{<t}).
        This IS the likelihood of the data under the model — not a label loss.
        """
        logits = self.forward(x[:, :-1])   # (B, T-1, V) — predict next from context
        targets = x[:, 1:]                  # (B, T-1) — next tokens as targets
        return nn.functional.cross_entropy(
            logits.reshape(-1, logits.size(-1)), targets.reshape(-1)
        )

    @torch.no_grad()
    def sample(self, prompt: Tensor, max_new_tokens: int = 50) -> Tensor:
        """
        Ancestral sampling: x_t ~ p(x_t | x_{<t}) for t = T+1, ...
        This is generation — the core capability that discriminative models lack.
        """
        x = prompt.clone()
        for _ in range(max_new_tokens):
            logits = self.forward(x)[:, -1, :]   # (B, V) — last position
            next_token = torch.multinomial(torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1), 1)
            x = torch.cat([x, next_token], dim=1)
        return x
Many generative models introduce a latent variable z that represents a compact, abstract description of x. The joint is p(x, z) = p(x | z) · p(z), and the marginal is obtained by integrating out z:

This integral is generally intractable — evaluating p(x) requires integrating over all possible z values. VAEs handle this via a variational lower bound (ELBO); diffusion models handle it by defining the latent z as a sequence of noised versions of x. Generation is done by sampling z ~ p(z) and then decoding x ~ p(x | z).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch import Tensor


class GaussianMixtureGenerative(nn.Module):
    """
    Simplest latent variable generative model: Gaussian Mixture Model.
    p(x) = sum_k pi_k * N(x; mu_k, Sigma_k)
    where z = k (the mixture component) is the discrete latent variable.

    p(x) = sum_k p(z=k) * p(x | z=k)

    Generation: sample z ~ Categorical(pi), then x ~ N(mu_z, Sigma_z).
    Classification via Bayes' rule: p(z | x) = p(x | z) p(z) / p(x).
    """
    def __init__(self, n_components: int = 5, dim: int = 2):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_components = n_components
        self.log_pi = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(n_components))    # log mixing weights
        self.means = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(n_components, dim))
        self.log_stds = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(n_components, dim))

    def log_prob(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """Evaluate log p(x) = log sum_k pi_k * N(x; mu_k, sigma_k)."""
        log_pi = self.log_pi - torch.logsumexp(self.log_pi, dim=0)  # normalise
        stds = self.log_stds.exp().clamp(min=1e-4)
        # (B, K) log p(x | z=k) for each component
        log_px_given_z = torch.distributions.Normal(
            self.means, stds
        ).log_prob(x.unsqueeze(1)).sum(-1)  # (B, K)
        # log p(x) = logsumexp_k [log pi_k + log p(x | z=k)]
        return torch.logsumexp(log_pi.unsqueeze(0) + log_px_given_z, dim=1)  # (B,)

    def sample(self, n: int, device=None) -> Tensor:
        """Sample x ~ p(x) by ancestral sampling: z ~ pi, x ~ p(x|z)."""
        log_pi = self.log_pi - torch.logsumexp(self.log_pi, dim=0)
        z = torch.multinomial(log_pi.exp(), n, replacement=True)   # (n,)
        means = self.means[z]                                        # (n, d)
        stds = self.log_stds.exp()[z]                               # (n, d)
        return torch.randn_like(means) * stds + means               # (n, d)
Sample generation: draw new x ~ p(x) or x ~ p(x | y = c) for a given class c. This is the defining capability — text generation, image synthesis, audio synthesis.
Density estimation: evaluate p(x) for any input, enabling anomaly detection (low p(x) = unusual), compression (high p(x) = compressible), and likelihood-based model comparison.
Missing data handling: because p(x) is modelled, computing p(x_observed) by marginalising over missing dimensions is principled.
Semi-supervised learning: unlabelled data can be used to improve p(x), which in turn helps p(y | x) through Bayes' theorem.
Inpainting and completion: p(x_missing | x_observed) = p(x) / p(x_observed) — a conditional derived from the generative model.
The Harder Objective: Why Generative Models Struggle at Classification

To classify via a generative model, compute p(y | x) ∝ p(x | y) · p(y) for each class y and pick the highest. This requires a high-quality model of p(x | y) — essentially a separate model of what images of cats look like vs what images of dogs look like. A single mistake in modelling the data distribution (e.g., overestimating the likelihood of blurry images for one class) directly corrupts the classifier. In contrast, a discriminative model only needs to capture the features that differ between classes, ignoring everything else.

Ng & Jordan (2002) showed formally that naive Bayes (a generative classifier) reaches its asymptotic error faster with fewer training examples, but logistic regression (discriminative) achieves lower asymptotic error with enough data. Generative models win in the low-data regime; discriminative models win when data is abundant.
Modern Generative Models Are Foundation Models

Large-scale generative pre-training has become the dominant paradigm in AI. GPT models are trained on the objective of maximising log p(x) over internet-scale text — a purely generative objective. The resulting model learns rich representations that can be steered for discriminative tasks through prompting, fine-tuning, or RLHF. This reverses the traditional classification picture: rather than choosing discriminative over generative for efficiency, practitioners train a single large generative model and adapt it to any downstream task.

Mental model: a generative model is a world simulator — it has internalised enough about the data distribution to conjure new examples that are statistically indistinguishable from real ones. A discriminative model is a classifier — it knows how to separate categories but has no internal model of what each category looks like. The generative model can answer 'show me a cat'; the discriminative model can only answer 'is this a cat?' Modern large language models are generative simulators of text whose simulation is rich enough that they can answer both kinds of question.

The distinction between generative and discriminative models is not merely academic — it determines what a model can do. A discriminative classifier that achieves 99% accuracy on image classification cannot synthesise a single realistic image, detect whether an input is out-of-distribution, or reason about what features make an image plausible. These capabilities require modelling p(x), the data distribution itself, and that requires a generative model. As AI moves from narrow classification toward general-purpose systems that can create, reason under uncertainty, and adapt to new tasks without retraining, generative models become the central paradigm.

Practically, understanding generative models clarifies why large language models are so general-purpose. A language model trained to maximise log p(x) over internet text must model every aspect of language — grammar, facts, reasoning, style, code, math. The discriminative alternative would require labelling each capability separately, which is impossible at scale. The generative objective is the only feasible way to build models that generalise broadly without task-specific supervision.
Draw new x ~ p(x) or x ~ p(x|y=c) for a given condition. This is the defining capability: text generation, image synthesis, audio synthesis, molecule generation.
Evaluate p(x) for any input, enabling anomaly detection (low p(x) = unusual), compression (high p(x) = compressible), and likelihood-based model comparison via held-out log-likelihood.
Because p(x) is modelled, computing p(x_observed) by marginalising over missing dimensions is principled. No imputation heuristics required.
Unlabelled data can improve p(x), which in turn helps p(y|x) through Bayes' theorem. Generative models make labelled-data-efficient classification possible.
p(x_missing | x_observed) = p(x) / p(x_observed) — a conditional that follows from the generative model without additional training.
Ng and Jordan (2002) showed generative classifiers (Naive Bayes) reach their asymptotic error faster than discriminative ones with limited labelled data. Generative models win when labels are scarce.

Maximising log p(x) is strictly harder than maximising log p(y|x). The model must explain every dimension of x — pixel values, texture, lighting — not just the features that differ between classes. This requires more data and compute.
For latent variable models (VAE, diffusion), p(x) = integral p(x|z)p(z)dz is intractable. Training requires surrogate objectives (ELBO, score matching) that are lower bounds, not the true likelihood.

With abundant labelled data, discriminative models (fine-tuned BERT, ResNet classifiers) outperform generative classifiers. The generative model wastes capacity modelling aspects of x irrelevant to the classification decision.

Evaluating a generative model requires human assessment (FID, CLIP score for images) or log-likelihood estimation (perplexity for language). Unlike classification accuracy, these metrics are noisy and task-dependent.

A model that can generate any content in the training distribution may produce harmful text, misinformation, or synthetic media for misuse. Discriminative models are inherently narrower and therefore easier to control.
When to Use Generative vs Discriminative
Real-World Example: Anomaly Detection in Manufacturing

A semiconductor fab uses a normalising flow (an explicit generative model) to model the distribution p(x) of images from a wafer inspection camera during normal production. After training on 50,000 defect-free wafer images, the flow assigns a log-likelihood to each new image. Images with log-likelihood below a threshold (more than 3 standard deviations below the training mean) are flagged as anomalies for human review. This approach requires no labelled defect examples — the generative model learns what 'normal' looks like and flags deviations. A discriminative approach (CNN classifier: defect vs. no defect) would require thousands of labelled defect images, which are rare and expensive to collect in early production.

# Anomaly detection using a simple autoencoder as an approximate generative model.
# High reconstruction error = low p(x) = anomaly.
import torch, torch.nn as nn

class AnomalyDetector(nn.Module):
    """
    Autoencoder-based anomaly detector: train on normal data only.
    At inference, flag inputs with high reconstruction error as anomalous.
    This is an approximation to generative-model anomaly detection.
    """
    def __init__(self, input_dim=784, bottleneck=32):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, 256), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(256, bottleneck),
        )
        self.decoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(bottleneck, 256), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(256, input_dim), nn.Sigmoid(),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.decoder(self.encoder(x))

    def anomaly_score(self, x) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Per-sample reconstruction error (MSE). High = anomalous."""        with torch.no_grad():
            x_hat = self(x)
            return ((x - x_hat) ** 2).mean(dim=1)  # (B,)


# Training: only on NORMAL examples
model = AnomalyDetector(input_dim=784)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

normal_data = torch.rand(1000, 784)  # placeholder: real wafer pixel data
for epoch in range(50):
    x_hat = model(normal_data)
    loss = nn.functional.mse_loss(x_hat, normal_data)
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

# Inference
normal_test = torch.rand(10, 784)
anomaly_test = torch.rand(10, 784) * 2 + 1   # out-of-distribution

scores_normal = model.anomaly_score(normal_test)
scores_anomaly = model.anomaly_score(anomaly_test)
threshold = scores_normal.mean() + 3 * scores_normal.std()

print("Normal (should all be False):",  (scores_normal > threshold).tolist())
print("Anomaly (should all be True):", (scores_anomaly > threshold).tolist())
Confusing generative model with generative AI.

A Gaussian mixture model is a generative model. A Naive Bayes classifier is a generative classifier. 'Generative AI' in the popular press refers specifically to large-scale neural generative models (LLMs, diffusion models). The term 'generative model' is older and broader.
Using log p(x) from a VAE as a calibrated density.

The ELBO is a lower bound on log p(x), not the true log-likelihood. Comparing ELBOs across different VAE architectures is only meaningful if the bound tightness is similar. Use importance-weighted AutoEncoder (IWAE) for a tighter bound.
Forgetting that generative classifiers can be discriminative at inference.

A model trained generatively (maximising log p(x,y)) can be used discriminatively at inference (computing p(y|x) via Bayes). The training objective is generative; the inference procedure is discriminative.
Evaluating image quality with pixel-level metrics.

MSE between generated and real images does not correlate with perceptual quality. Use FID (Frechet Inception Distance) or IS (Inception Score), which compare distributions of deep features rather than pixel values.
Choose the model family based on what you need from p(x).
Tractable likelihood? Use autoregressive or normalising flows. Latent structure for interpolation? Use VAE. Best sample quality? Use diffusion. Fastest training? Use GAN (but harder to stabilise).
Measure held-out log-likelihood for model selection.
For explicit-likelihood models, use bits-per-dimension (bpd) on a held-out test set as the primary metric. Lower bpd = better density model.
Use FID/IS for generative image model evaluation.
FID (lower is better) compares the distribution of generated images to real images in Inception feature space. It correlates better with human judgement than log-likelihood for pixel-level models.
For production generation systems, implement content filters post-model.
Generative models produce the full support of the training distribution, which may include harmful content. Discriminative classifiers applied as post-generation filters are the standard mitigation.

The training cost of generative models varies enormously by family. A Gaussian mixture model (GMM) with 10 components in 2D fits in milliseconds on CPU. A VAE on MNIST (784 dimensions, z_dim=20) trains in 5 minutes on a single GPU. GPT-3 (175B parameters) required approximately 3,640 petaflop-days of training compute. The scaling law is roughly: compute ~ model_params × training_tokens, and performance on downstream tasks improves smoothly as both scale.

Inference cost also scales: autoregressive models are sequential (O(T) decoding steps for a length-T output), while diffusion models require multiple denoising steps (typically 20-1000 forward passes). For high-throughput production, batch all requests and use quantisation (INT8/FP16) to maximise GPU utilisation.
Probabilistic graphical models, VAE via SVI

Generative models can be abused to synthesise harmful content: deepfake images, disinformation text, synthetic voice audio for fraud. Any system deploying a generative model in production must implement output classifiers (discriminative models) that filter generated content before it reaches users. This is the standard architecture: generative model produces candidates, discriminative classifier screens for policy violations.

From a reliability perspective, generative models can exhibit mode collapse (GANs generate limited variety) or hallucination (language models generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect text). Reliability mitigations: factual grounding via retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), output confidence calibration, and post-generation factual verification via discriminative classifiers trained on known-false statements.
Generative models represent p(x) or p(x, y), enabling sampling, density estimation, and principled handling of missing data — capabilities absent from discriminative models.
Training maximises marginal log-likelihood log p(x), which is a strictly harder objective than the conditional log-likelihood log p(y | x) used by discriminative models.
Three families: explicit likelihood (autoregressive, flows), approximate likelihood (VAE, diffusion), and implicit likelihood (GAN, EBM), each with different sampling and training trade-offs.
Generative models win in the low-data regime and for semi-supervised learning; discriminative models win when labelled data is abundant and the task is classification.

Large-scale autoregressive language models (GPT family) are generative models that have become general-purpose foundation models — their generative objective produces representations strong enough for discriminative tasks via fine-tuning or prompting.
